title: The West End
html:
By 8:53 AM Jiang Wenxu had been observing the west-end figure at intervals for approximately 100 minutes and it had not moved. Not once. The east-wing figures had defined patrol rhythms — they moved, stopped, turned, held position, cycled back. The west-end figure stood where it stood and did nothing else. The distinction mattered because behavior with a pattern has gaps in it, and behavior without one doesn't.

He relayed this to Song Yu in two sentences.

Song Yu's response was to look at the window gap, look at the corridor door, and say: "So it's been standing there since you woke up." Not a question.

"Since before I woke up," Jiang Wenxu said. "I can't see the grounds from inside 7-C. The first time I checked the window it was already there."

Song Yu processed this. "It could react to movement. It just hasn't had any movement to react to on the west side."

"Possible." He'd considered this. The east-wing figures had reacted to the Squeak! of Song Yu's shoe at 22 seconds, not sufficiently to break their positions but enough to register the sound. The west-end figure had been standing 8 meters from a stairwell door. Anyone trying to reach that door would pass within 6 or 7 meters of it. Whether standing still in its line of sight was an option depended entirely on whether its trigger was movement, proximity, or something else he hadn't identified yet.

He needed more data and the only way to get it was to go look.

The west side of the corridor was not the east wing. Item 7 specified east-wing doors; the 30-second knock rule didn't apply over here, or at least didn't apply with the same guarantee. The doors on the west side he hadn't tested. He hadn't needed to. The west corridor doors nearest to 7-C were 7-B and 7-A, both untaped, both closed when he'd looked earlier. The stairwell door at the far end, by the layout, would be somewhere between those and the fire exit.

He stepped out of 7-C and turned west for the first time since he'd arrived.

The west corridor had the same institutional green walls and the same cracked plaster at the corners as the rest of the building, but the lights here ran in a different pattern: the fixture immediately above the staff figure at the far end was the dark one he'd spotted through the door gap in chapter one. Everything else was on and even. The figure stood 30 meters away at the end of the corridor, back to him, exactly as it had been through the window boards: white uniform, wrong neck, motionless.

Song Yu came out of 7-C behind him and stopped at the doorframe.

[He turned WEST. The whole time I thought he was going east again]

[The stationary one is at the far end. He's walking toward it]

[Walking. Not sneaking. Just. Walking.]

[I need to lie down]

[Stream count is at 200 and climbing. The algorithm found this somehow]

He got to 7-B first. Plain handle, no tape, label above: 7-B. He kept moving and reached 7-A, which was the last door before the staff figure and the stairwell beyond it. The door of 7-A was not quite closed. A gap of approximately 2 cm at the frame edge, just enough that the interior was not sealed from the corridor. From inside: breathing. Fast and shallow, the respiration of someone in a sustained high-anxiety state who has been in it long enough to normalize the rate without being able to lower it.

He stopped.

Song Yu's footsteps behind him also stopped. Jiang Wenxu heard him get closer without coming all the way forward, stopping about a meter back. "Someone in there," Song Yu said, very quietly.

"Yes."

"Leave it. We don't know what it is."

He looked at the door of 7-A. No tape on the handle. He looked at the staff figure at the end of the corridor, which had not moved. He looked back at the door. The gap between the door and the frame was 2 cm and the breathing on the other side of it was fast but it was human-fast, the specific unsteady rhythm that fear produces rather than the mechanical evenness of the staff figures when they stood at rest.

Item 2: do not enter rooms with red-tape handles. Room 7-A had no tape on the handle. The item didn't cover untaped rooms. Whoever was inside 7-A had been there since before Jiang Wenxu woke up — the breathing was too established, too settled into its own rhythm, to belong to someone who'd arrived recently. They'd been in that room for hours and hadn't triggered anything that had ended them. Whatever item 2 was protecting against, the hazard it described was specific to the taped rooms.

He knocked on the door of 7-A. 3 times, the same way he'd knocked on 8-B. Not loud.

The breathing on the other side of the door stopped.

[He knocked again. He knocked on ANOTHER door]

[Song Yu looks like he's deciding whether to physically stop him]

[This is different from the east wing. Item 7 is east-wing doors only]

[So why is he knocking]

[Because someone is in there and they're scared and he can hear them breathing??]

[That's. Huh. That's actually why, isn't it]

He counted to 30. The door didn't open by itself the way 8-B had. At 30 seconds the gap between door and frame was the same 2 cm it had been before. Whoever was inside had the door braced from their side and was not going to open it on a count.

"It's not east wing," he said quietly, toward the gap. "Item 7 doesn't apply here. I'm not a staff figure. My name is Jiang Wenxu. I'm standing in the corridor."

Four seconds of silence. Then: "How many." The voice was young — late teens, male, roughened by what sounded like several hours of not using it.

"Two." He glanced back at Song Yu.

Another pause. The door swung inward.

The person inside was a boy, 16 or 17, in a school uniform — dark trousers and a white collared shirt that had been worn through a full day and then a night in this building, the collar wrinkled and damp at the back, the trouser knees dusty from the floor. He was pressed against the wall just inside the door with both hands flat at his sides, using the wall to hold himself upright in a way that had probably become unconscious. His face was pale in the particular way of someone who'd gone past the acute stage of fear into whatever the body settles into after hours of it.

He looked at Jiang Wenxu. He looked at Song Yu and the pipe. He looked at Jiang Wenxu again.

"You're players," the boy said.

"Yes," Jiang Wenxu said.

"How long have you been here."

"About 2 hours."

The boy's expression shifted slightly. "I've been here since yesterday evening," he said. "They selected me at 6 PM."

Song Yu spoke from behind Jiang Wenxu's shoulder, voice still at corridor-quiet. "You survived from 6 PM yesterday."

"I stayed in this room. I didn't go into the corridor until—" He stopped. "I went out once. Early this morning. Before the lights came back on."

"What happened," Jiang Wenxu said.

The boy's jaw tightened. "There were more of them. I got back in here and shut the door and didn't go out again." He looked at his hands. "I heard someone running this morning. Around 7 AM."

The adjacent player from the corridor. 7:22 AM.

Song Yu said, from the doorway, not stepping inside: "His name's what?" He directed this at the boy.

"Chen Shu." He added, a half-second later: "I'm in year 11."

Song Yu didn't say anything to this. His opinion of year-11 players in survival instances was visible in approximately the same way that Jiang Wenxu's opinions were usually invisible, which was clearly and without attempt at concealment.

"You were here overnight," Jiang Wenxu said.

"Yes."

"Item 1. After 10 PM. What changes."

Chen Shu looked at him and for the first time the sustained blankness in his face shifted into something more specific. "You read the sign," he said.

"Yes."

"There are more of them at night. A lot more. And they don't just patrol, they—" He stopped again, pressing his back against the wall harder. "They look for sounds. Any sound. Even breathing, if you're too close to the corridor."

Item 1's qualifier: after 10 PM, before 6 AM. The bracket existed because at night, the building's behavior changed significantly enough to require a different set of rules. The daytime rules were a restricted subset of the nighttime rules, which was the correct way to read a sign that covered both periods. He filed this.

"You survived by not making any sound after 10 PM," he said.

"And before 10." Chen Shu looked at the door gap. "The one in the corridor outside doesn't move. I've been watching it through the gap since this morning. I thought if I didn't make a sound it would stay where it was."

[Okay so the kid has ALSO been watching the west-end figure]

[He's been here the whole night and he survived by doing basically what the new player has been doing. Not moving. Not making noise.]

[Two people independently landed on the same strategy and neither of them talked to each other until now]

[Song Yu is not happy about this third person. That face.]

[Yeah because Song Yu spent 2 hours trying to run corridors and nearly died 3 times lol]

Song Yu stepped into the room far enough to pull the door to. "We're wasting time," he said, still quiet. "We have the key. We know the lobby is ground floor via the west stairwell. The stairwell door is 8 meters past the figure outside. We need to go, and we can't take a—"

"What's behind the stairwell door," Jiang Wenxu said.

Song Yu stopped.

"I mean: does the stairwell door have a lock. Does the key open it, or does the key only open the front desk once we're in the lobby."

Song Yu didn't answer immediately, which was itself an answer. He hadn't been to the stairwell door. His 2 hours had all been spent in the east wing.

Chen Shu said: "It opens."

Both of them looked at him.

"The stairwell door. It doesn't have a lock on this side. I checked it this morning when I went out. It's just a push bar. But I came back because—" He glanced at the corridor through the door gap. "Because of that."

The stairwell door was a push-bar. They didn't need the key to get through it. They needed to get past the figure outside and descend to the ground floor lobby, and the key was for whatever lock was on the front desk itself.

Jiang Wenxu looked at the 2 cm gap and the motionless white-uniformed back visible in the far end of the corridor. Chen Shu had checked the stairwell door at some point this morning, which meant at some point this morning he had been within 6 or 7 meters of the west-end figure and it hadn't reacted. That was one data point. The conditions at that time were different from now — lighting, time of day, whatever else shifted between overnight and morning — and one data point didn't establish a pattern.

But it was more than he'd had 10 minutes ago.

Song Yu was looking at the door gap too, then at Chen Shu, then at Jiang Wenxu, waiting to see what the next move was going to be with the expression of someone who had stopped generating his own plans in the last 40 minutes and was instead watching someone else's take shape, which was not a comfortable position to be in for a person on his third instance.

The survival probability notification had not changed.
